This is a common misunderstanding. Cache is ever-growing. Nothing is ever deleted from cache. Deleting expired keys isn't part of dogpile.cache's API and never will be. This is why I don't to store binary or whole HTTP requests in cache. With the amount of requests done by subliminal that would mean a really fast paced growing cache.

Only some backends do cache clean up of expired keys based on the backend expiration time which is different from dogpile.cache's.
Documentation says backend's expiration time should be greater than dogpile.cache's but I think a lesser value would trigger a cache refresh, if that's what you're looking for.

I have found a way to make this work without going through these hoops:
Override ```_prep_app``` in your ```dashcast``` controller, so that it always kills the running app.
Add a ```backdrop_ready``` flag to ```cast_listener```.
In the overridden ```prep_app```, kill the running app and wait for the flag to be set.

I know this is a bit creative, but it seemed like the least weird solution. At one point I considered putting the `stream` object inside the controller object, but decided against it, as I think this solution makes the `CastController` classes themselves more concise. I'd also like to preserve the explicit nature of current `cli.py`.

It actually depends on for what kind of action the callback is required. It depends of the code in the callback function.

In my case it was when launching an app and error is on google cast device side so it should not close the connection (so return 0). It happened on some case when an application is locked on the cast or if app id is not found.
The 'new_launch_error' listener should implement the logic to handle the correct behavior when it happens but this is not a socket issue.

Maybe in some other cases, the cause is more serious and could require disconnection but I'm not really an expert in this.
My guess would be to never disconnect.

I also believe that this could explained some previous cases I experienced when the callback failed (for whatever reason) and was causing loss of control with pychromecast. 
It could be judicious to show in the logs the underlying/parent exception for debug purpose. The `self.logger.exception('runonce: error calling callback')` will not give enough information.

An alternative way would be remove the exception raised on this part as it's not really relevant in that case (it's just a regular error status and not an exception).
```
# original
def _block_till_launched(self, app_id):
        if self.blocking:
            self.app_launch_event.wait()
            if self.launch_failure:
                raise LaunchError(
                    "Failed to launch app: {}, Reason: {}".format(
                        app_id, self.launch_failure.reason))

# just logging instead of raising exception
def _block_till_launched(self, app_id):
        if self.blocking:
            self.app_launch_event.wait()
            if self.launch_failure:
                self.logger.error(
                    "Failed to launch app: {}, Reason: {}".format(
                        app_id, self.launch_failure.reason))
```

It turns out it's ok here because we're
handling references, so whatever the evaluation order, in the end we get an int and a reference to a list.

If we evaluate from left to right, we first update the list then return
a reference to this list.

if we evaluate from right to left we first return a reference then
evaluate then update this reference.

From a call site point of view, we get an int and a reference, and the
reference ultimately points to the updated list ;-)

Agreed. I think it's fine as is. You could probably write it as a map, but the simplest thing I can come up with is:

```python
self.annotation_filter = set()
map(lambda include_name: self.annotation_filter.update(STANDARD_ANNOTATIONS[include_name]), include)
```
which is considerably worse. I mean, you could also just do:
```python
self.annotations = set()
for include_name in include:
    self.annotations.update(STANDARD_ANNOTATIONS[include_name])
```

This has the nice property of not allowing `include` values that aren't in `STANDARD_ANNOTATIONS`, and is way more readable. Doesn't feel as pythonic, though.

I tried looking into it. I was running on a machine where with default configuration's of 10 threads I was getting 100MB+, but I was never able to get a single thread (with bandwidth enabled or not enabled) to ever reach that. It was seeing speeds of ~25 MB with bandwidth turned off but turning on to something high resulted in the same speeds. I saw this for both uploads and downloads. I want to see it on your environment though.

As to the bandwidth calculations, those are just bursty in general because we aggregate the amounts we report back so if only one thread is working it will report in increments of that threshold. When there is more threads reporting it smoothes the progress updates more because there are more threads forcing the the progress to update. So that may explain that? If the threshold is low and you have one thread working, I can see the progress even more bursty because there is only thread reporting and it has to do sleeps.

I wasn't asking to duplicate these tests. test_heap.py is enough to test the heap implementation itself.

For this file, your assumption should be that the heap implementation works as expected. You only need to do a sort of sanity check that PeerHeap's own operations work, and that it does what we say HeapOperation does.

Like, make sure that `pop_peer()` doesn't explode when the list is empty. Stuff like that. Basically, test_heap gives us confidence in the heap implementation and test_peer_heap gives us confidence that PeerHeap knows how to work with that heap implementation without blowing up.

The only way out of the above loop is to retry issues `self._offsets_fetch_max_retries` number of times. Why would it ever be valid to continue retrying something `self._offsets_fetch_max_retries` number of times in order to finally fall out of the loop?

The code should not repeatedly retry what is a non-error condition.

What this change does is within the loop check for the valid condition of zero reqs needed, due to having no partitions assigned to this consumer, and cleaning return from within the loop.

If the code does get to the end of the maximum number of retries and falls out of the loop, then it is an error. Why else would `self._offsets_fetch_max_retries` have been attempted?

Is this possible?
```
try:
    extensions = app.extensions
except AttributeError:  # Sphinx<1.6
    extensions = app._extensions
```
First trying `app._extensions` means that by default we're still accessing Sphinx internals and this could break at any time, e.g. if the Sphinx guys decide to change the private `app._extensions` in the future.

I do think there _could_ be a single global `Client` (config), but language would have to be moved to the `Query` instead, since that is state that varies per-call. I wanted a logger with a good format. Very useful for development and debugging. But `siphon`  does a call to `basicConfig`:

> This function does nothing if the root logger already has handlers configured for it.

and this code is needed to remove existing handlers (otherwise you get two traces with different handlers/formatters). See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12034393/import-side-effects-on-logging-how-to-reset-the-logging-module

Another option is to add the logger code before the `siphon` imports, but that is not pep8 compliant.

I guess that, for the shake of clarity, I can use the basic config set up by `siphon`:

```python
import logging
from siphon.radarserver import RadarServer
from siphon.cdmr import Dataset

logger = logging.getLogger('nexrad_copy')
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
logger.info('Foo Bar')
```

```
INFO:nexrad_copy:Foo Bar
```

Yes, for this test case we should skip it right now. Here's the reasoning:
- It was using the implicitly created service principal that we cache under ~/.azure/acsServicePrincipal.json
- that SP wasn't being cleaned up by the tests
- we are all way too familiar with the problems that can happen with ~250 SPs attached to an account: your subscription is basically paralyzed and the items aren't deletable by reasonable means
- for historical reasons, the explicit --service-principal flag isn't supported by `az acs create --orchestrator-type=Kubernetes` so the decorators we use in the other test cases won't work

Until I figure out a better approach, it seems safer not to leak Service Principals with every test run.

It is a bit scary. My favored approach (please see https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/2182#issuecomment-172099220 and the following comment) is to do away with conditional dependencies.

The biggest use-case I can think of for keeping the same key is to be able to use the [HTTP Public-Key-Pins header (HPKP)](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7469).

With HPKP, a server needs to have at least 2 public keys listed: one for the current certificate, and at least one backup key that should not be stored on the server. I would want to continue using the currently active key to ensure that someone who visits my site and sees my HPKP header just before I renew my certificate under normal circumstances does not get locked out of my site for any length of time. If the key rotates all the time (Even if every 60-90 days), then there wouldn't be a safe way to use HPKP with lets encrypt (The suggested length of time for the HPKP max-age is 60 days, which is close to how often a server operator would need to rotate their lets encrypt certificate).

Hi Robert, for the 2 places where contains switch statements, they both have a case condition `protocol::MessageType::DisconnectClient` which does not belong to most of other cases from `object_manager_protocol::MessageType`. If I use `static_cast` in `switch`, then I need to use `static_cast<MessageType>(CommonMessageType::DISCONNECT_CLIENT)` which will cause error because there is no 0 in `MessageType`. Therefore, there are 2 options to solve this problem. The first one is the way I used in my code change. The other way is to add an enum element which equals to 0 in local_scheduler.fbs and object_manager.fbs. Do you think the second way is better?

@ericl As for the actual algorithm, you're right this is a change in behavior from before. I think that before, statistics were only being collected once in a while (one in every hundred times, that's what the `calc_obstat_prob=0.01` value was), returned to the master, aggregated, and set on all the workers. Now we update the observation filter with every step but we don't share the values with the other workers. The upsides are that it's simpler to implement, though it does make things more "stateful". I'm guessing it doesn't hurt performance too much.
